title: Blood in Cat Urine (Hematuria): Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn what to do if you see blood in your cat's urine, the urgency level, and why a photo of the litter box helps your veterinarian with triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Blood in cat urine typically requires a veterinary appointment within 24 hours, but a total inability to urinate is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate ER transport.

What does it mean if there is blood in my cat's urine?

Blood in a cat's urine, known clinically as hematuria, can manifest as a light pink tint, bright red spots, or even small dark clots in the litter box. It is a sign that there is inflammation, irritation, or injury occurring somewhere in the urinary tract, most commonly the bladder or the urethra.

Is blood in my cat's urine a veterinary emergency?

  • Medium Urgency: Most cases are caused by Feline Lower Urinary Tract Disease (FLUTD) or a urinary tract infection, requiring a vet visit within 24 hours.
  • Life-Threatening Emergency: If your cat is straining and unable to pass any urine at all, this indicates a blockage and requires immediate transport to an emergency veterinarian.

How does taking a photo of the urine help with my cat's triage?

  • Allows the veterinary team to assess the concentration of blood in the urine.
  • Helps identify the presence of mucus, crystals, or clots.
  • Gauges the severity of the inflammation to determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen.
  • Assists the vet in deciding which diagnostic tests will be required for your visit.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats presenting with hematuria, potential causes include feline lower urinary tract disease (FLUTD), urinary tract infection, neoplasia, trauma, urethral plugs, urolithiasis, and sterile cystitis. Urolithiasis, with common uroliths consisting of calcium oxalate, magnesium ammonium phosphate, and urate, should be suspected in cases of hematuria, dysuria, or urethral obstruction. Urinalysis, urine culture, radiography, and ultrasonography are essential to differentiate uroliths from other conditions. Radiography, cystoscopy, or ultrasonography are critically important to detect uroliths, as abdominal palpation alone has low sensitivity. Radiographic evidence of uroliths is seen in a minority of cats with hematuria or dysuria. In cases of neoplasia, history and clinical signs are highly suggestive; urinalysis frequently reveals hematuria, and neoplastic cells may be found in the sediment, particularly with transitional cell carcinomas.

Chapter: Urology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1531)

faqs.question: Can stress cause blood in my cat's urine?
faqs.answer: Yes, stress is a leading trigger for Feline Idiopathic Cystitis (FIC), which causes bladder inflammation and bloody urine even without an infection.

faqs.question: What if my cat is acting normal otherwise?
faqs.answer: Even if your cat is eating and playing, blood in the urine indicates discomfort and an underlying issue that needs medical attention to prevent it from worsening.

faqs.question: How will the vet diagnose the cause?
faqs.answer: Vets typically perform a urinalysis to check for bacteria, crystals, and pH levels, and may recommend an ultrasound or X-ray to check for bladder stones.
